results.title: BBB study finds most AI-related customer reviews are negative | Local News | ktvo.com
results.content:
The organization also reported receiving nearly 100,000 AI and automation-related complaints, reviews and scam reports over the past three years.

The study found many consumers felt confused, ignored or unable to resolve issues when interacting with automated systems.

Complaints were especially common in online retail, e-commerce and technology-focused industries.

> "People's main complaint is that they want to have an opportunity during the customer service experience or transaction to talk to a human, and I think what's most frustrating for them is when that doesn't happen," said Kesha Duncan, regional director for the Better Business Bureau. [...] BBB officials said consumers frequently expressed dissatisfaction with businesses that relied heavily on automation without providing a clear path to a human representative.

Many reported difficulties resolving problems, escalating complaints or receiving personalized assistance.

The report found AI-related complaints and reviews have increased steadily in recent years.

The BBB recorded more than 22,000 complaints mentioning AI or automation in 2023, more than 25,000 in 2024 and nearly 34,000 in 2025.

While the organization acknowledged that AI can help businesses improve efficiency, it recommends companies maintain opportunities for customers to speak with a person when needed. [...] You have permission to edit this article.

results.title: Free AI Complaint Letter Generator - Firm, Polite Emails
results.content:
Use a billing dispute tone when money, invoices, subscriptions, or incorrect charges are involved. Keep it factual and record-like.

Use an escalation tone only when you need to reference prior attempts and set a clear next step. Even then, avoid insults or threats that make the message harder to resolve.

## Example input and generated complaint structure

Input:

> I ordered two lamps on March 4. One arrived broken on March 8. I emailed support on March 9 with photos and have not heard back. Order #48391. I want a replacement or refund.

A strong generated complaint should include:

 A specific subject line
 A short opening that identifies the order
 A factual description of the issue
 A brief timeline
 A clear requested resolution
 A note about available evidence

results.title: communication - What is the best (or at least appropriate) way to deal with "AI slop" messages, emails and requests? - The Workplace Stack Exchange
results.content:
Once you see that this is the kind of request you received, skim it quickly to identify one question that looks relevant. Respond with a short answer to that. Just send it, and let them follow up with additional questions if they actually have them. Put the ball back in their court. Don't mention what you didn't understand, unless you think it's critical to the task.

When people respond in this way to a well thought out email with multiple important questions in it, it's frustrating. But one of your problems here is that you don't know what the important questions are, hidden behind the "wall of text". Make a quick guess, respond with something that might be helpful, and let them do the work of highlighting what's important to them, if they wish. [...] First of all, talk with your manager about how these AI slop emails are disrupting your work, and establish some form of prioritization. After that, consider these requests as very big, very complex and very long unscheduled and not prioritized projects, as they are, and respond with something as:

> Hi,
>
> The text sent is very long. The length of it and overall structure indicates that it will take a long time to analyse it, make corrections, and then answer or clarify any questions it contains.

If is the case in your line of work, as suggested by Stephan Kolassa answer, add:

> Please reply with a booking number to a [project budget][cost center].

If not:

> I will later talk with my boss about prioritization of this task before taking it.

And finally: [...] Then as people complain you direct them to the long list of other requests and their requestors who are taking up all your precious time. The goal being to get the requestors to fight with each other claiming their work is higher priority until some kind of arrangement comes down that limits the amount of AI slop they can dump on you.

### Ignore the requests until they follow up

Create a Follow-Up folder in Outlook and take all those random requests as they came in and toss them in there and ignore them. For some (hopefully small) percentage of them people will send a follow up email requesting status on a previous request. At that point work the request. It is amazing how many requests that there will be no follow up on since the request never had any value to begin with.

Share

results.title: What Are the Most Common Complaints About AI Customer Support Tools? | Twig
results.content:
How to avoid it: Choose AI platforms that integrate deeply with your existing support stack. When your AI can pull customer history from Zendesk, order data from your commerce platform, and previous conversation context from your CRM, it can provide personalized, context-aware responses.

## Complaint 4: Responses Feel Robotic and Scripted

Customers can tell when they are talking to a poorly implemented AI. Overly formal language, generic responses that do not address the specific question, and rigid conversation flows all create an experience that feels more frustrating than helpful. [...] Key takeaways:

 Hallucinated or inaccurate answers are the number one complaint driving customer frustration
 Poor escalation to human agents traps customers in unhelpful AI loops
 Lack of context forces customers to repeat themselves across interactions
 Rigid, scripted responses fail to address nuanced customer issues
 Twig addresses these complaints through source-attributed accuracy and smart integration design

## Complaint 1: The AI Gives Wrong or Made-Up Answers

This is the most damaging complaint and the most common. AI hallucination, where the model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information, is the fundamental trust-breaker in customer support. [...] When a customer asks about your return policy and gets a fabricated answer, the consequences ripple outward. The customer acts on bad information. They become frustrated when reality does not match what the AI told them. They lose trust in your brand. And they tell others about their experience.

According to Gartner, accuracy concerns remain the primary barrier to customer acceptance of AI in service interactions. Customers would rather wait for a human than get an instant wrong answer.

Why it happens: Many AI tools use general-purpose language models without adequate grounding in your specific business data. The model fills gaps in its knowledge with plausible-sounding but incorrect information.

results.title: The unintended consequences of writing your emails with AI – Digital Society Blog
results.content:
Emotional dissonance, by contrast, refers to a tension that workers feel when there’s a mismatch between the emotions they truly experience and those they are expected to display (Wharton, 2009).  Consider the following scenario: a student misses several classes in a row and then, without offering any valid explanation, asks me to elaborate on some of the concepts that were covered in their absence. In such cases, it may be beneficial – for both me and the student –if the tone of my email reflects some of the frustration I feel, rather than opting for the more convenient and bland email reply that AI generates. Why? Because for me as the lecturer, there is value in expressing my emotions and in communicating authentically– studies indicate that emotional dissonance is associated with [...] ## Dissonances

A second unintended consequence of using AI for your emails is that it can trigger dissonance both on the side of the sender and of the receiver. In a broader sense, dissonance refers to “a lack of harmony or agreement between things”. Two types of dissonance may occur in this context: cognitive dissonance and emotional dissonance. Let’s begin with an example that illustrates the experience of cognitive dissonance. [...] Despite the benefits of AI-mediated communication, there may be unintended consequences of using AI for emails. To start with the obvious: colleagues and clients can get easily annoyed by AI’s formulaic and stilted tone. This phenomenon is so widespread that experts recommend workers to add a personal touch to their AI-generated email. In addition, there is the paradoxical effect that by answering emails faster, we may end up with more emails to write and answer. But there are other more subtle and yet concerning consequences of using AI for emails: deskilling, dissonances, and detachment.

## Deskilling
